summary: Gruber breaks down Apple's iPhone 12 lineup across all four models, analyzing pricing, camera differences, and product strategy. He argues the lineup is well-structured despite having four models, with the Mini's name being self-explanatory and prices more continuous than before. The camera differences between 12 Pro and 12 Pro Max represent a return to the era when larger phones had exclusive camera features, which frustrates those wanting the best camera in a smaller body. He critiques the heavy-handed 5G marketing as gratuitous, questions Apple's decision not to make a non-Pro Max model, and defends the removal of chargers and earbuds from the box. The piece also notes this was the first flagship iPhone launch without Phil Schiller.
key_points: The iPhone 12 lineup's pricing is far more continuous than 2019's, with the LCD-to-OLED gap eliminated now that all models use OLED — the $300 chasm between iPhone 11 and 11 Pro shrinks to $120
The 12 Pro Max has meaningful camera advantages over the 12 Pro (larger sensor, sensor-shift OIS, longer telephoto), breaking the pattern where Max was just a bigger version of the same phone

Apple strategically withholds large displays from non-Pro products across iPhone, iPad, and MacBook lines to protect premium pricing — a non-Pro iPhone 12 Max would be popular but would cannibalize Pro Max sales
The 5G marketing was heavy-handed and gratuitous, with Verizon's CEO getting four conspicuous minutes on stage despite LTE speed not being a real consumer pain point
Removing chargers and earbuds is defensible — they were never free, and savvy buyers who don't need them are effectively getting a discount
money_quotes: The addition of a fourth iPhone, the 12 Mini, doesn't complicate the lineup at all, because its name completely describes everything there is to know about it.
Apple's promotion of the iPhone 12 and 12 Mini as starting at $799/699 rather than $829/729 just because of some sort of marketing deals they cut with AT&T and Verizon is $30 worth of bullshit.
You don't have to be Jeff Williams to figure out that OLED displays are expensive.
You can't simultaneously clamor for a smaller device and a bigger battery.
The future is clearly not iPhones with USB-C ports instead of Lightning, but iPhones with no ports at all, like Apple Watch.
Going from 2G EDGE to 3G was a breakthrough in performance. 3G to LTE was significant. But today, when it comes to complaints and wishes for your phone, is 'LTE is not fast enough' even on your list?

People are complaining about Apple pinching pennies by no longer including the power adapter and headphones with new iPhones, but to me, the most notable omissions across the board are entire products that don't exist: non-Pro iPhones, iPads, and MacBooks with large displays.
As a general rule in life, it's better to pay for what you need.
key_insight: Apple's most significant product decisions aren't about what's in the box but about which products don't exist — strategically withholding large displays from non-Pro models across every product line to protect premium pricing.
